relations: Black holes are formed from the remnants of massive stars that have undergone a supernova explosion
Black holes contain a singularity at their center
The event horizon surrounds the singularity at the center of a black hole
Black holes cause gravitational time dilation due to their immense gravitational pull
The Schwarzschild radius defines the size of a black hole
General relativity's breakdown at the singularity of black holes indicates the need for new physics to describe these phenomena
Quantum gravity is sought to explain the phenomena of black holes beyond the scope of general relativity
Hawking radiation is a phenomenon associated with black holes, predicting their ability to emit radiation and eventually evaporate
The holographic principle is a concept developed from the study of black holes, challenging traditional views on information storage.
The information paradox is directly related to the nature of black holes and the fate of information that falls into them.
The detection of gravitational waves was evidence of black holes' existence, resulting from the merger of two black holes.
Black holes serve as a unique testing ground for the theories of general relativity.
The study of black holes has inspired new ideas in quantum mechanics.
The study of black holes has contributed to new ideas in string theory.
The LIGO observatory was responsible for the detection of gravitational waves, confirming the existence of black holes.
Quantum gravity is speculated to extend the principles of general relativity to provide a more complete description of black holes
Hawking radiation was predicted by Stephen Hawking, linking his work directly to this phenomenon
The prediction of Hawking radiation leads to the black hole information paradox, as it suggests information could be lost when black holes evaporate
